revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
{{Authority control}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Minamoto no, Yoriyoshi}} [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:Nobility from Kyoto]] [[Category:988 births]] [[Category:1075 deaths]] [[Category:Japanese nobility]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]]

He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
{{Authority control}}

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
{{Authority control}}

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>

==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]

Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qqq" to "qq"
revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. {{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
{{Japan-mil-hist-stub}} {{japan-noble-stub}}

revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Minamoto|lang=Japanese}} [[File:源頼義.jpg|thumb|228px|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi]] {{Nihongo|'''Minamoto no Yoriyoshi'''|源 頼義||988 - August 27, 1075}} was a Japanese [[samurai]] lord who was the head of the [[Minamoto clan]] and served as ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]''. Along with his son [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], he led the Imperial forces against rebellious forces in the north, a campaign called the [[Zenkunen War]], which would be followed some years later by the [[Gosannen War]].<ref name=Sansom>{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A history of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |ISBN=0804705232 |pages=249–251}}</ref>
== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
{{Japan-mil-hist-stub}} {{japan-noble-stub}}

== Biography == His childhood name was Odaimaru (王代丸). He held the title, passed down from his father, of ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'', Commander-in-chief of the Defense of the North.
Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>

==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.

Yoriyoshi accompanied his father [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]]<ref name=Sato>{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |ISBN=9781590207307 |pages=74–75, 83–85, 95–109}}</ref> on his own missions to defend the Empire, quelling rebellions and disturbances. Thus he gained much of his knowledge of tactics and strategy. He fought in the Zenkunen War for twelve years starting in 1051, including the [[Battle of Kawasaki]].<ref name=Turnbull2>{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co. |year=1998 |ISBN=1854095234 |page=199}}</ref> In 1063, Yoriyoshi founded [[Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachiman-gū]] in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]]<ref>[http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html 鶴岡八幡宮] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090201102318/http://www.hachimangu.or.jp/about/lineage/index.html |date=2009-02-01 }} "History of Tsurugaoka Hachiman Shrine" (in Japanese) retrieved on February 4, 2009</ref> which was to become, roughly a century later, the primary shrine of the Minamoto clan when they began the [[Kamakura shogunate]].
His son was [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]], who "would go on to be admired by his contemporaries as the greatest warrior."<ref name=Sato/>
In 1065, he ordained as a Buddhist monk and received the [[Dharma name]] '''Shinkai''' (信海).
==Family== * Father: ''[[Chinjufu-shōgun]]'' [[Minamoto no Yorinobu]] * Mother: Shuri no Myobu * Wife: daughter of Taira no Naokata * Children: ** [[Minamoto no Yoshiie]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshitsuna]] ** [[Minamoto no Yoshimitsu]]
==See also== * [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū]] * [[Hachiman]]
==References== {{Reflist}}
==Sources== *Sansom, George (1958). 'A History of Japan to 1334'. Stanford, California: Stanford University Press.
